rulings.Case Title: EUGENE RUDNITSKY et al VS. QI YUAN HU et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Terminating Sanctions For Failure To Appear
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 31, 2015, Line 2, Defendant Qi Yuan Hi's Motion For Terminating Sanctions For Failure To Appear (Part 1 of 2 For Purposes of Entry of Tentative Ruling Only) Pro Tem Judge Mark Schreiber, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: MOTION DENIED WITHOUT PREJUDICE. THE LAWYER SUBMITTING THE MOTION, LORI R. MAYFIELD OF THE LAW OFFICE OF LORI MAYFIELD, IS NEITHER THE ATTORNEY OF RECORD IN THIS MATTER (STEPHEN PLAS) NOR SHOWN ON THE MOTION AN ATTORNEY WITH THE LAW FIRM OF THE ATTORNEY OF RECORD (THE LAW OFFICES OF BRIAN MCCLELLAN); THE COURT REGISTER OF ACTIONS SHOWS NO RECORD OF MS. MAYFIELD FILING ANY ASSOCIATION OR SUBSTITUTION OF COUNSEL FOR DEFENDANT IN THIS MATTER. CCP SECTION 128.7; SEE ALSO, ELLIS LAW GROUP, LLP V. NEVADA CITY SUGAR LOAF PROPERTIES, LLC (2014) 230 CA4th 244, 258. AS TO THE MERITS OF THE REQUEST FOR TERMINATING SANCTIONS, THE MOTION, EVEN THOUGH UNOPPOSED, APPEARS DEFICIENT IN THAT THERE IS NO SHOWING WHY LESSER SANCTIONS, SUCH AS ISSUE OR EVIDENTIARY SANCTIONS, WOULD NOT BE AN ADEQUATE REMEDY FOR THE NON-COMPLIANCE WITH THE PRIOR DISCOVERY ORDER IDENTIFIED IN THE PAPERS AND THE OTHER DESCRIBED INSTANCES OF NON-COOPERATION IN DISCOVERY. DISMISSAL IS A PROPER SANCTION TO PUNISH THE FAILURE TO COMPLY WITH A RULE OR AN ORDER ONLY IF THE COURT'S AUTHORITY CANNOT BE VINDICATED THROUGH THE IMPOSITION OF A LESS SEVERE ALTERNATIVE. (E.G., LYONS V. WICKHORST (1986) 42 CAL.3d 911, 917; SEE ALSO, WILSON V. JEFFERSON (1985), 163 CAL.APP.3d 952, 958 (TERMINATING SANCTIONS FOUND EXCESSIVE EVEN THOUGH MOTION UNOPPOSED AS THE COURT MUST STILL CONSIDER WHETHER LESSER SANCTIONS WOULD BE SUFFICIENT.) ALSO, THE COURT REGISTER OF ACTIONS DOES NOT SHOW THE FILING OF ANY PROOF OF SERVICE OF THE DISCOVERY ORDER AND NO SUCH PROOF WAS PROVIDED WITH THE MOTION. (Tentative Ruling Continued in Part 2 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: HRD COFFEE SHOP INC. VS. RICHARD TOM
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Preliminary Injunction And Preliminary Injunction
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, July 31, 2015, Line 10, PLAINTIFF HRD COFFEE SHOP INC., DAVID YEUNG'S Preliminary Injunction And Preliminary Injunction. Denied. Plaintiffs have not established a probability of prevailing, irreparable harm, or that they do not have an adequate remedy at law. Plaintiffs admit that they entered into an agreement with Defendants Richard Tom and Joanna Banks to form Bingowah LLC. (First Amended Complaint ("FAC"), par. 9; Declaration of David Yeung, par. 10.) Plaintiffs also admit that they licensed the name, logo, and appearance of HRD, as well as trade secret information to Bingowah LLC to open up a second HRD location. (Yeung Decl., pars. 10-11; FAC, 9, 12.) Plaintiffs argue in their moving papers that they are likely to prevail in demonstrating that Defendants are infringing upon their trade name and trade dress. However, Plaintiffs admit that they gave Defendants express permission to use their trade name, trade dress, and recipes to open up a second location. Therefore, Plaintiffs have not established a probability of prevailing on their claim that Defendants are infringing upon their trade name and trade dress. Additionally, Plaintiffs have not established irreparable harm. Plaintiffs admit that Defendants locked David Yeung out of the business in early 2013. (FAC, par. 14; Yeung Decl., par. 15.) Plaintiffs did not file suit against Defendants until March 2015 and did not file a motion for preliminary injunction until June 2015. It can be inferred from the evidence that HRD Grill operated continuously through this time period. Plaintiffs do not explain why they waited over two years to move for a preliminary injunction. This significant passage of time strongly suggests that Plaintiffs will not be irreparably harmed if an injunction is not issued until after a trial on the merits in this action is concluded. Lastly, Plaintiffs have not established they do not have an adequate remedy at law. Plaintiffs allege that they agreed to allow Bingowah to use HRD's name and recipes in exchange for equal sharing of the profits. (FAC, par. 12-13.) Plaintiffs allege that Defendants breached the agreement by refusing to share profits with Yeung. (FAC, pars. 14-15.) Money damages will compensate Plaintiffs for this alleged breach. Nowhere in the FAC do Plaintiffs allege that the parties agreed that Yeung has a contractual right to participate in the operation of HRD Grill. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EG)

rulings.Case Title: MISSION STREET SF, LLC VS. CROWDFLOWER, INC. ET AL (Pltf. 170.6 Challenge Judge Quidachay)
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Crowdflower, Inc.'S Demurrer To Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, July 31, 2015, Line 12, Defendant Crowdflower, Inc.'s Demurrer To Complaint. The court overrules the demurrer to the first cause of action [expiration of fixed term lease]. Under Ryland v. Appelbaum (1924) 70 Cal.App. 268, defendant needed to vacate after the expiration of the fixed term. The complaint does not allege any facts showing that plaintiff accepted defendant's rent after the expiration or otherwise consented to a continued occupation. The court sustains the demurrer to the second cause of action [illegality] without leave to amend. A comprehensive reading of Rowe v. Wells Fargo Realty Services, Inc., (1985) 166 Cal.App.3d 310, indicates that a tenant's violation of a statute or ordinance will support an eviction only where the usage (1) threatens physical safety; (2) stigmatizes the premises; or (3) impairs the landlord's ability to receive rent. Here, par. 12 of the lease states that the premises will be used for "office space." The 3-day notice states that defendant is illegally using the premises as "an Administrative Services office." It is unclear whether defendant is violating the lease as the premises is being used as an office. Moreover, there are no facts (and plaintiff does not cite any in the opposition to warrant leave to amend) that show how defendant's use threatens physical safety, stigmatizes the property, or impairs plaintiff's ability to receive rent. "Unless the lease specifically limits the use of the property to a particular purpose, or that restriction is necessarily inferred from the language which is employed, the lease may not be forfeited on account of the mere use of the property for another purpose even though that be an illegal use prohibited by statute, for the reason that forfeitures of leases are not favored by the law. But under such circumstances the lessor will be compelled to resort to a suit for damages or for injunctive relief to compensate him or to prevent a continued unauthorized use of the premises." Keating v. Preston (1940) 42 Cal.App.2d 110, 115. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EG)
